candidate_problems.text: How can 'identifiable through predictive necessity' be formalized and tested? The conjecture hinges on persistence, breadth, and compression advantage, but these are still intuitive. A key open problem is to define measurable criteria under which a latent variable is the uniquely simplest reusable explanation for predictive uncertainty, and to distinguish this from cases where multiple non-isomorphic internal representations predict equally well.

What properties of the training distribution and model class determine whether shortcuts dominate over real invariants? The conjecture attributes success or failure not to next-token prediction alone but to its coupling with environment structure. An important unresolved tension is how dataset diversity, duplication, context length, noise, and architecture/optimizer biases interact to make invariant-learning or shortcut-learning the easier attractor.

When prediction succeeds via an internal representation, in what sense can that representation be said to align with a 'real domain invariant' rather than merely being an equivalent predictive code? The conjecture assumes a distinction between genuine invariants and sufficient statistics, but leaves open the ontology and identifiability of latent variables under representational equivalence. A new problem is to specify what counts as alignment with reality beyond predictive performance and compression alone.
